intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users feel less intimidated by prep steps if ingredients disappear from view as they're added, creating a sense of progress and simplification
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and behavior — progressive disclosure through animated removal vs. static checklist
facade.label: Ingredients vanish
facade.acceptImplies: User values visual feedback that transforms complexity into simplicity; appreciates animation that creates narrative momentum through the cooking process
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers persistent reference points and static lists; may find animations distracting or gimmicky when trying to focus on actual cooking tasks

facade.hypothesis: Users of a hate-cooking app will prefer persistent, always-visible navigation over tab switching, reducing cognitive load when jumping between recipes, shopping lists, and pantry inventory.
facade.label: Sidebar nav
facade.acceptImplies: User values persistent context and quick access to multiple sections without modal switching. App should feature a fixed left sidebar with category buttons.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers tab-based or modal navigation. Suggests screen real estate concerns or preference for focused, single-task views. Consider bottom tabs or hamburger menu instead.

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, a cozy but stripped-down interface—minimal steps, zero clutter—reduces friction without feeling cold or sterile.
facade.axisTargeted: Warm vs cool + Organic vs digital
facade.label: Warm, minimal kitchen
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward approachable, human-scaled design that doesn't overwhelm. Comfort matters as much as simplicity.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer either more personality/visual richness, OR colder efficiency (pure utility). Warmth without minimalism, or minimalism without warmth, might resonate more.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer interfaces that feel approachable and unstressful, with high contrast and lots of white space rather than dark or cluttered designs
facade.axisTargeted: Light vs dark / Density of visual information
facade.label: Bright & minimal
facade.acceptImplies: Explore clean, airy layouts with high contrast; test light backgrounds, sans-serif type, and generous spacing; assume users want visual calm and quick scans
facade.rejectImplies: Test darker palettes, richer textures, or denser information density; perhaps this audience wants more visual personality or coziness than pure minimalism offers

evidence.hypothesis: Users feel less intimidated by prep steps if ingredients disappear from view as they're added, creating a sense of progress and simplification
evidence.implication: User values visual feedback that transforms complexity into simplicity; appreciates animation that creates narrative momentum through the cooking process

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer minimal friction — they want to see WHAT to make before committing to HOW. A single, bold recipe name with no description, ingredients list, or time estimate creates immediate clarity and reduces cognitive load.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (full recipe upfront) vs. Key metric (recipe name only)
facade.label: Just the recipe title
facade.acceptImplies: Users want progressive disclosure — show them the goal first, then details on demand. The app should prioritize recipe discovery over recipe completeness in the initial view.
facade.rejectImplies: Users need confidence before engaging. They want to see time, difficulty, ingredients, or a photo alongside the title to decide if a recipe is worth opening.

facade.hypothesis: A voice that's witty and self-aware about the cooking struggle—making fun of kitchen complexity rather than pretending it's easy—will feel more honest and less intimidating to people who hate cooking
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and personality — playful vs serious
facade.label: Sarcastic robot
facade.acceptImplies: Users want personality that validates their frustration. They're drawn to irreverent, knowing tone over cheerleader positivity. App can lean into humor and cynicism about cooking.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer straightforward, judgment-free guidance without comedy. They want to feel capable and smart, not mocked. App should be warm and encouraging, not sarcastic.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking are more likely to engage with step-by-step actions if call-to-action buttons animate into view at the right moment, rather than being static—creating anticipation and signaling 'you're ready for the next thing'
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — fluid, moment-driven interactions vs. static, always-available UI
facade.label: Buttons slide in
facade.acceptImplies: User values motion as a guide through process; appreciates UI that feels alive and responsive to their progress; may respond well to other gesture-based transitions and status feedback
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers predictable, stable UI; finds animated buttons distracting or slow; wants all options visible upfront without waiting for animations to complete

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 20 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so easy, you'll actually cook them

Filter by mood
Today's picks
Garlic butter pasta
8 min
4 ingredients Stovetop only
Scrambled eggs on toast
5 min
3 ingredients Breakfast
Tuna mayo sandwich
3 min
5 ingredients No cooking
